item.title: Nvidia is supposedly "trying to hide" the RTX 5060, as there may not be reviews at launch
item.itunes:title: Nvidia is supposedly "trying to hide" the RTX 5060, as there may not be reviews at launch
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Nvidia is releasing the highly anticipated RTX 5060 during Computex.
  • Reviews might be delayed due to a driver, limiting early assessments.
  • Concerns surround the 8GB RTX 5060 due to its frame buffer size.

Just a couple of weeks before Computex kicks off, Nvidia made a surprise announcement that its much-anticipated RTX 5060 would launch during the annual event. The GPU goes on sale on May 19, just a day before Computex begins officially, and during a time when the vast majority of the tech press will be on the ground in Taiwan. Despite board partners already shipping cards out to reviewers, Nvidia says it won't provide a driver for testing the RTX 5060 until the card releases, according to Ignor's Lab, essentially locking out day-one reviews.

Related
3 reasons why the RTX 5060 will be dead on arrival

Despite some promising rumors of elevated stock and reasonable pricing, Nvidia still haven't done enough to make the 5060 worth buying.

7

We might not see any RTX 5060 reviews on release day

Between the largest computing event of the year and a lagging driver, it's not looking good

Igor Wallossek, the namesake of Igor's Lab, made the situation clear. Despite having access to RTX 5060 review units, Nvidia has yet to provide the outlet a driver to perform the review. And while waiting for the driver to show up by May 19, Wallossek will be in Taiwan for Computex and won't be able to return to test the card until May 26. It's tough to say what the situation is elsewhere, as any reviews are certainly under an embargo until a day before (or the day of) the RTX 5060's official release.

Still, Igor's Lab isn't alone. Popular YouTube channel HardwareUnboxed alleges that Nvidia is "trying to hide" the RTX 5060, pointing to the recent release of the 8GB RTX 5060 Ti that wasn't widely available to customers nor widely seeded to reviewers. Hardware Unboxed says it has "multiple RTX 5060 samples" from Nvidia's board partners, but it doesn't expect to review them until a week after the card goes on sale.

Despite traditionally being the most popular class of Nvidia graphics cards among PC gamers, the RTX 5060 has already found itself embroiled in controversy. The upset mainly concerns the card's 8GB frame buffer, which has been widely criticized as being inadequate for the demands of modern games, even at 1080p. It seems that PC gamers are making their voices heard on that front, too. Despite a decent success for the 16GB RTX 5060 Ti, the cheaper 8GB variant has reportedly seen poor sales since release.

Although the RTX 5060 launches during Computex, we might not hear much about the card from Nvidia. In recent keynote addresses, Nvidia's gaming-focused launches have been a footnote in presentations largely focused on AI advancements. Thankfully, Nvidia's board partners will be at the show and likely eager to show off their new GPU models.

item.title: 6 free and open-source apps you actually haven't heard of
item.itunes:title: 6 free and open-source apps you actually haven't heard of
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The world of tech is often dominated by huge corporations constantly finding new ways to monetize their users, whether it's by directly charging users or by collecting and potentially selling their data. But in the midst of all that, there are a ton of fully free, open-source apps that can help you in your everyday life without those downsides. And there's a very good chance you've never heard of them.

So let's talk about it: here are some great free and open-source apps — and for this article, we're talking about ones you've almost certainly never used. There's also a lot of self-hosted software on this list, so these are fun projects to set up in the first place.

6 Mixxx

The name may be misleading

Despite what the use of the triple X may suggest, Mixxx is simply a music-making app; or more specifically, a DJ mixing app. Now, as you can probably guess, I'm not a musician, but Mixxx appears to be a very capable platform. It starts out by importing your music library (which you can point it to), so you can easily start adding tracks and mixing them together as needed.

You can then add all kinds of instruments, including multiple microphones and other dedicated hardware that allows you to create increasingly complex tracks. It also has built-in recording so you can record your mixes for publishing, or you can link up a dedicated streaming service to share your work online in real time.

Admittedly, I can't truly begin to grasp the potential of this app, but there's a lot you can do here and the UI is surprisingly intuitive for something so complex. With a little bit of guidance, you'll be off to the races pretty quickly. And again, this is an open-source app, so you can find the source code here, and it's totally free.

  • Mixxx
    See at Mixxx See at Github

5 Persepolis

A download manager that makes your life easier

Most people might not even think they need a download manager, but Persepolis is a fantastic app that shows exactly why you might want to have one. This cross-platform download manager runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows (where I did most of my testing), and it offers a lot of features you might not have even considered you need. You can easily find and queue multiple downloads and start them whenever you're ready. Not only does Persepolis parse typical download links, but you can point to locations such as YouTube to download videos, too, so this is already a huge feature to have.

But aside from the ability to manually pause and start downloads so you can handle them later, Persepolis has a proper scheduling function so you can choose when downloads should start and end, including the ability to force your PC to stay awake to finish the downloads. Persepolis can also import multiple download links in one go using a text file, so if there's a set of apps you always install on a PC,m, for example, you can have a text file with all the URLs you need and fire off all the downloads in one go.

There's quite a lot to like here, and of course, you can find the source code easily to verify it's safe. The app triggered a trojan virus warning on my Windows machine, but it seems to be a false positive due to the package being automatically built by GitHub Actions.

  • Persepolis
    See at Github

4 Pairdrop

Share files locally and privately

Sharing files locally is something you might not even think of doing in the age of the internet and cloud storage, but there are still apps out there that allow you to do just that, and PairDrop is one of them. PairDrop is available on the web as a standard website, but if you want to be even more local, you can also self-host a PairDrop server. You can send pretty much any kind of file over PairDrop, and even send text messages to other devices.

There isn't much of a difference between the self-hosted and online implementations, aside from the fact that your devices won't be discoverable outside your network if you're using a local server, even using a link or group code. Of course, self-hosting always has more of a sense of accomplishment and an extra layer of privacy, so it's a fun option to try if you're feeling so inclined.

  • Pairdrop
    See at Pairdrop See at Github

3 Ntfy

Notifications for everything

When you start playing around with smart homes and a lot of self-hosted services, one of the things that can come in handy is setting up notifications for various kinds of events. For example, if you use a monitoring tool like Uptime Kuma, you can set up notifications for important events regarding the services you're monitoring.

Ntfy is a conceptually simple but very capable notification service that can basically integrate with almost any service that uses HTTP notifications so that you can receive these notifications on your phone. This makes it that much easier to keep up with all the information you care about from sensors and services that you choose to connect, even if you're not on your main PC or away from home.

  • Ntfy
    See at Github

2 Vert

A file conversion tool that's all yours, on any device

It happens too often that I download a video or audio file that I can't open properly on my computer. Generally, this is less of a problem on macOS for me, but Windows isn't compatible with a lot of modern codecs without using a third-party app, so converting files to a more universally-accepted format is a great tool to have. And that's exactly what Vert is.

This image, video, and audio converter is available on the web, but the source code is publicly available, and even better, you can host it yourself on your own server if you don't want to have to rely on the internet. Actually, the standard web version already converts audio, images, and documents locally on your device, but if you want absolute privacy for converting your videos, the self-hosted approach is perfect for you.

A ton of formats are supported for conversion, so you can create a file that works pretty much anywhere. On top of that, Vert has no limits on file size or paywalls, something you can't say about a lot of free online conversion tools. This one is definitely worth checking out.

  • Vert
    See at Vert See at Github

1 KitchenOwl

Your personal, private cookbook

I'm not much of a cook (yet), but the idea of organizing my favorite recipes does sound very appealing, and keeping them backed up in case their original sources go down would be great. That's where KitchenOwl comes in. KitchenOwl is a personal, self-hosted cookbook where you can store all your recipes, keep track of available ingredients, and create shopping lists so you can be sure you have everything need to make a specific recipe or meal. KitchenOwl lets you import meals from a wide range of supported websites, with options to modify recipes as needed.

That's not even all of it, either. KitchenOwl also includes meal planning so those with busier lives can plan ahead and know what to eat and when so there's no stress thinking about it every day. Plus, there's an expense tracker built-in, which you can use for basically anything you want, not just food. It's a great home management solution, and having it hosted on your personal server makes total sense, though KitchenOwl also operates as a web-based tool if you want to go that route.

  • KitchenOwl
    See at KitchenOwl See at Github

Start exploring open-source apps

These are just a few of the many open-source apps out there that you (or even I) have never heard of and that are worth checking out. While I'm not personally going to need some of these, others definitely get my interest directly. KitchenOwl and PairDrop are probably apps I'm going to keep loaded on my NAS going forward, and I can fully see Vert staying there, too. I recommend checking these out if you'd like to try out some new apps that can greatly enhance your digital life.

item.title: Alienware is changing everything you know about its gaming laptops
item.itunes:title: Alienware is changing everything you know about its gaming laptops
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Alienware's new laptop range is named Aurora and starts at $1,149.
  • Alienware is focusing on portability and power balance with the Aurora range.
  • New Aurora laptops feature unique design focused on airflow and performance

Alienware, and Dell more broadly, is in a state of transition, and the company's gaming laptops are next up on the docket. After overhauling Dell laptops with a new naming scheme, and reintroducing the Area-51 desktop, Alienware is changing up its range of laptops with the new Aurora range. In addition to new hardware, including Arrow Lake CPUs and RTX 50-series GPUs, the Aurora range comes with a redesigned chassis that focuses on portability.

Related
Alienware just embarrassed everyone with its 4K OLED monitor

Great performance, but an even better price.

3

Alienware's new laptop range is named Aurora

And models start at just $1,149

Previously, Alienware had two ranges of laptops. M-series laptops were focused on performance while X-series laptops were focused on portability at the cost of power. Going forward, Alienware will use the already announced Area-51 name for its flagship devices and use Aurora as its "versatility tier." There's a focus on portability, but Alienware is calling the design "streamlined" now. Previous X-series devices would sacrifice quite a bit of power to reach their portable form factor, and it seems the new Aurora range is trying to nail a better balance.

The big change is Alienware's new AW30 design, which debuted on Area-51 laptops earlier this year. Just like their bigger siblings, the new Aurora laptops come with soft, rounded corners around all edges and Alienware's Cryo-Chamber cooler design. It's a small bump under the laptop that sits it up at a slight angle, providing better surface area and more airflow to the internal components without sacrificing the thickness of the laptop.

More models in the Aurora range will arrive soon, but out of the gate, Alienware has the 16 Aurora and 16X Aurora. Both come with up to an RTX 5070 mobile GPU, but everything else about them is slightly different. For the 16X, you can pack in up to 64GB of DDR5 memory, 4TB split across two drives, and up to an Intel Core Ultra 9 275HX processor. The 16X also offers 115W to the GPU, along with a 16-inch QHD+ display that tops out at 500 nits of brightness with a 240Hz refresh rate.

The base 16 Aurora is a touch thinner and lighter, and it comes with several differences in the hardware. The GPU runs at 85W, for instance, and the laptop tops out at a Core 9 270H CPU. Alienware also trimmed back to a single SSD with up to 2TB of capacity, a 120Hz display with 300 nits of brightness, and up to 32GB of DDR5 memory.

Select configurations of the new Aurora laptops are available now, starting at $1,149. Alienware will have the new laptops on display at PAX East in Boston from May 8 to 11, as well.

item.title: Review: Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
item.itunes:title: Review: Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Quick Links

  • Pricing, specs, and availability
  • What I liked about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
  • What I didn't like about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
  • Should you buy the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3?

Android tablets have been in a strange place lately, with Samsung cornering the premium category, the budget category filled with eventual e-waste, and the middle ground ceded to the iPad. The only place to get smaller screens is in the budget category as well, since the Nexus range is gone, and anyone who wants a smaller tablet that's actually good is forced to get the iPad mini.

That's now changing, as the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 comes with flagship specs, a compact chassis, and is everything that an Android fan could want from a smaller slate. This 8.8-inch screen has a gorgeous, fast IPS display, is powered by the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, and has plenty of RAM and storage for running the latest games. If you were wondering why you've not heard about it until now, it's because this device has largely only been available in China, but that's no longer the case.

I've been using it for media consumption, gaming, and even a bit of work thanks to the desktop mode, and it's performed well. I'm still not convinced it needed a 165Hz refresh rate screen, especially since most Android games are limited to 120Hz, but it's bright and colorful, and best of all—doesn't use PWM dimming, so it hasn't caused any headaches.

About this review: Lenovo loaned us a Legion Tab Gen 3 for the purposes of this review. The company had no input in this article, and did not see its contents before publishing.

Small, speedy Android slate
Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3

Finally we have a Nexus 7 successor

8 / 10
$490 $550 Save $60

The Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 is a 8.8-inch Android tablet with a fast 165Hz refresh rate IPS screen, and plenty of grunt from the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 SoC. The smaller screen makes it more usable for daily use, and it's gorgeous when playing games. Oh, and Lenovo includes a heat-dispersing folio case, glass screen protectors, and a 68W fast charger.

Pros & Cons
  • Excellent build quality
  • Wi-Fi 7 support
  • Plenty of accessories included
  • Limited console-quality games
  • No expandable storage
$490 at Lenovo

Pricing, specs, and availability

Lenovo launched the Legion Tab Gen 3 at CES 2025, and it sold out within 48 hours. At launch, it was $500, but it quickly got a price increase to $550, which seems to be the new MSRP. It's on sale already, down to $490. It's got flagship specifications, although it's a last-generation flagship, with the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 chipset, 12GB of RAM, and 256GB of storage. There's a second version in China with 512GB of storage, but only the 256GB option is available globally. The only place to get it currently is directly from Lenovo.

Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
Storage
256 GB UFS 4.0
CPU
Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 Processor
Memory
12 GB LPDDR5X 8533 MHz
Operating System
Android 14
Battery
6,550mAh
Ports
Two USB-C with pass-through charging
Camera (Rear, Front)
Rear: 13 MP AF + 2 MP (macro) with flash Front: 8 MP FF with flash
Display type
8.8-inch 2.5K (2560 x 1600), LTPS, Touch, 500 nits, 165Hz
Price
Starting at $490
Size
8.21x5.10x0.31in (208.54x129.46x7.79mm)
Connectivity
Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 5.4
Headphone jack
No
Colors
Eclipse Black
Expandable Storage
No
Weight
0.77lbs (350g)
Refresh rate
165 Hz

What I liked about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3

This speedy slate is also small enough to slide into my pocket

Let's start with the obvious. Even though Lenovo's Legion brand is gamer-centric, the Legion Tab Gen 3 mercifully lacks any gamer design. The camera module is about as close as it gets on the exterior, and even then it echoes many popular Android phones. The Legion Space app for quickly getting back to your games is more gamer-y, but you don't need to use it because it doesn't control anything useful, unlike how much you needed it on the Legion Go handheld.

is everything that an Android fan could want from a smaller slate

Although the matte-black exterior is classy enough, it's a fingerprint magnet, so you'll want to use the folio case Lenovo provides to keep it pristine. Inside, there's the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 paired with 12GB of LPDDR5X RAM, 256GB of storage, and a 6,550mAh battery. This is rated for about 16 hours of video playback at 200 nits brightness (roughly 33% of maximum), although that number will be lowered if you're gaming. You also get Wi-Fi 7 and Bluetooth, a 13MP main camera, a 2MP macro camera, and an 8MP front-facing camera.

The build quality is excellent, with a full-metal exterior. It's very solid in the hand, which will come in handy when gaming sessions get stressful. The 8.8-inch IPS display has a 165Hz refresh rate, brightness of 500 nits (and peak of closer to 900 nits), and a 16:10 aspect ratio for gaming. And you get dual USB-C ports, a 10GB/s one on the bottom and a 480MB/s port on the side that's used for pass-through power when gaming. When used, it'll power the tablet without engaging the battery, saving the battery's longevity.

Plenty of power for Android gaming

I've been using the Legion Tab Gen 3 primarily for media consumption and gaming, which it excels at. The tablet has a floating widget on the left edge that gives you quick options, like on a gaming handheld, and the primary use is to switch between battery-saving, balanced, and performance modes. As you can imagine, these adjust the frequency of the SoC to either save power or provide performance, but be warned that the performance mode uses lots of battery life while the tablet gets quite warm, and the battery-saving mode doesn't always give enough performance, leading to stuttering.

I've been playing Minecraft, Candy Crush, and a few other matching games, and they all perform brilliantly on the fast display. Plus, there's no PWM dimming in action, so there'sa smaller chance of getting a headache. It's a shame my Backbone controller isn't able to stretch open enough, because a mobile controller is still going to be the best way to play on this tablet; something like the Razer Kishi Ultra would be perfect.

Lenovo's UI makes split-screen use of two apps easy, although this only works with apps and not games, which still take up the entire screen. And there's also a desktop mode that lets you run apps as floating windows, which I found easy enough to handle productivity tasks that I struggle with on my phone's smaller screen. It's not really a replacement for a laptop, but at least you can get some work done at a pinch.

Lenovo included (almost) everything you could need

In a time when companies are trying to remove things from the included accessories, Lenovo stuffs the Legion Tab Gen 3 box with extras. You get a 68W fast charger, a USB-C to USB-C cable, a glass screen protector with tools for installation, and a folio case with a removable magnetic cover that helps vent extra heat from the tablet when used. I love seeing this, especially when cases or screen protectors might not always come from third-party vendors.

 Related
The Boox Note Air4 C is like an Android tablet combined with a Kindle, and it's everything I ever wanted

The idea of an Android-powered E Ink tablet changes everything

5

What I didn't like about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3

It's got some odd decisions and it gets quite hot during long gaming sessions

Lenovo gets quite a lot right with this tablet, but some odd choices are at play here. I'm entirely convinced that nobody needs a dedicated macro lens on a tablet, let alone on a smartphone. The included folio is a nice touch, but it doesn't wrap around the screen as nicely as it could, or fold as nicely into a kickstand. Admittedly, these are minor quirks, especially when the rest of the tablet is pretty awesome. Oh, and there's no fingerprint scanner or secure face unlock, which is disappointing as those are fantastic features for easy access when you want to game.

It's got enough screen space for gaming or productivity without becoming a burden to carry around, and it feels premium in the hand

There is one other concern, and it's one that's mitigated somewhat by the metal chassis and the vented folio case. This tablet gets hot to the touch after a short while when gaming, often over 45 °C in my testing. That's not nice to hold with your fingertips, again making the case for using this tablet with a controller.

I'm not convinced this display needed to be 165Hz

The 8.8-inch display is smooth, bright, and gorgeous, and suspiciously similar to the specs of the Legion Go gaming handheld. So similar that I wouldn't be surprised to find it's the same panel, except the touchscreen works much better than it did on the Windows-powered handheld so it might be slightly different.

Either way, that's the only reason I can think that a 165Hz refresh rate was used for an Android tablet, because the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 isn't powerful enough for higher FPS, and most Android games are hard-capped at 60Hz or 120Hz. The list of high refresh capable games is still relatively short, so Lenovo could have cut costs somewhat by using a lesser specification. Unless, of course, it was using up old stock from the Legion Go.

 Related
Lenovo Tab Plus review: A surprisingly great budget Android tablet

This Android tablet is much better than its price suggests

3

Should you buy the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3?

You should buy the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 if:

  • You loved the Nexus 7 and wanted its successor
  • You use your tablet to play games
  • You want the option for productivity tasks in desktop mode

You should NOT buy the Lenovo Legion Tab 3 if:

  • You need a long battery life
  • You want a larger screen for media or getting work done
  • You want a fingerprint sensor for security

I believe Android tablets are better when they're smaller, and the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 absolutely fits that bill. It's got plenty of power, a beautiful 8.8-inch screen with a higher refresh rate than you'll ever need, and can use a stylus for sketching and notetaking. It's even more usable now that the upgrade to Android 15 is here, and I feel this will be a firm favorite of Android fans.

It's even more usable now that the upgrade to Android 15 is here, and I feel this will be a firm favorite of Android fans.

It's got enough screen space for gaming or productivity without becoming a burden to carry around, and it feels premium in the hand. I'm not sure anyone buys tablets to use the cameras, but they're decent enough if you don't mind the odd color shift, and the audio system is loud and punchy. There is a successor on the horizon powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite chip, so you might want to wait to see how pricing shakes out, but for now, this is the best small-screen Android tablet you can get.

Small, speedy, Android slate
Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
8 / 10
$490 $550 Save $60

The Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 is a 8.8-inch Android tablet with a fast 165Hz refresh rate IPS screen, and plenty of grunt from the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 SoC. The smaller screen makes it more usable for daily use, and it's gorgeous when playing games. Oh, and Lenovo includes a heat-dispersing folio case, glass screen protectors, and a 68W fast charger.

$490 at Lenovo

Quick Links

  • Pricing, specs, and availability
  • What I liked about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
  • What I didn't like about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
  • Should you buy the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3?

That's now changing, as the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 comes with flagship specs, a compact chassis, and is everything that an Android fan could want from a smaller slate. This 8.8-inch screen has a gorgeous, fast IPS display, is powered by the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, and has plenty of RAM and storage for running the latest games. If you were wondering why you've not heard about it until now, it's because this device has largely only been available in China, but that's no longer the case.

I've been using it for media consumption, gaming, and even a bit of work thanks to the desktop mode, and it's performed well. I'm still not convinced it needed a 165Hz refresh rate screen, especially since most Android games are limited to 120Hz, but it's bright and colorful, and best of all—doesn't use PWM dimming, so it hasn't caused any headaches.

About this review: Lenovo loaned us a Legion Tab Gen 3 for the purposes of this review. The company had no input in this article, and did not see its contents before publishing.

Small, speedy Android slate
Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3

Finally we have a Nexus 7 successor

8 / 10
$490 $550 Save $60

The Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 is a 8.8-inch Android tablet with a fast 165Hz refresh rate IPS screen, and plenty of grunt from the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 SoC. The smaller screen makes it more usable for daily use, and it's gorgeous when playing games. Oh, and Lenovo includes a heat-dispersing folio case, glass screen protectors, and a 68W fast charger.

Pros & Cons
  • Excellent build quality
  • Wi-Fi 7 support
  • Plenty of accessories included
  • Limited console-quality games
  • No expandable storage
$490 at Lenovo

Pricing, specs, and availability

Lenovo launched the Legion Tab Gen 3 at CES 2025, and it sold out within 48 hours. At launch, it was $500, but it quickly got a price increase to $550, which seems to be the new MSRP. It's on sale already, down to $490. It's got flagship specifications, although it's a last-generation flagship, with the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 chipset, 12GB of RAM, and 256GB of storage. There's a second version in China with 512GB of storage, but only the 256GB option is available globally. The only place to get it currently is directly from Lenovo.

Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
Storage
256 GB UFS 4.0
CPU
Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 Processor
Memory
12 GB LPDDR5X 8533 MHz
Operating System
Android 14
Battery
6,550mAh
Ports
Two USB-C with pass-through charging
Camera (Rear, Front)
Rear: 13 MP AF + 2 MP (macro) with flash Front: 8 MP FF with flash
Display type
8.8-inch 2.5K (2560 x 1600), LTPS, Touch, 500 nits, 165Hz
Price
Starting at $490
Size
8.21x5.10x0.31in (208.54x129.46x7.79mm)
Connectivity
Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 5.4
Headphone jack
No
Colors
Eclipse Black
Expandable Storage
No
Weight
0.77lbs (350g)
Refresh rate
165 Hz

What I liked about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3

This speedy slate is also small enough to slide into my pocket

Let's start with the obvious. Even though Lenovo's Legion brand is gamer-centric, the Legion Tab Gen 3 mercifully lacks any gamer design. The camera module is about as close as it gets on the exterior, and even then it echoes many popular Android phones. The Legion Space app for quickly getting back to your games is more gamer-y, but you don't need to use it because it doesn't control anything useful, unlike how much you needed it on the Legion Go handheld.

is everything that an Android fan could want from a smaller slate

Although the matte-black exterior is classy enough, it's a fingerprint magnet, so you'll want to use the folio case Lenovo provides to keep it pristine. Inside, there's the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 paired with 12GB of LPDDR5X RAM, 256GB of storage, and a 6,550mAh battery. This is rated for about 16 hours of video playback at 200 nits brightness (roughly 33% of maximum), although that number will be lowered if you're gaming. You also get Wi-Fi 7 and Bluetooth, a 13MP main camera, a 2MP macro camera, and an 8MP front-facing camera.

The build quality is excellent, with a full-metal exterior. It's very solid in the hand, which will come in handy when gaming sessions get stressful. The 8.8-inch IPS display has a 165Hz refresh rate, brightness of 500 nits (and peak of closer to 900 nits), and a 16:10 aspect ratio for gaming. And you get dual USB-C ports, a 10GB/s one on the bottom and a 480MB/s port on the side that's used for pass-through power when gaming. When used, it'll power the tablet without engaging the battery, saving the battery's longevity.

Plenty of power for Android gaming

I've been using the Legion Tab Gen 3 primarily for media consumption and gaming, which it excels at. The tablet has a floating widget on the left edge that gives you quick options, like on a gaming handheld, and the primary use is to switch between battery-saving, balanced, and performance modes. As you can imagine, these adjust the frequency of the SoC to either save power or provide performance, but be warned that the performance mode uses lots of battery life while the tablet gets quite warm, and the battery-saving mode doesn't always give enough performance, leading to stuttering.

I've been playing Minecraft, Candy Crush, and a few other matching games, and they all perform brilliantly on the fast display. Plus, there's no PWM dimming in action, so there'sa smaller chance of getting a headache. It's a shame my Backbone controller isn't able to stretch open enough, because a mobile controller is still going to be the best way to play on this tablet; something like the Razer Kishi Ultra would be perfect.

Lenovo's UI makes split-screen use of two apps easy, although this only works with apps and not games, which still take up the entire screen. And there's also a desktop mode that lets you run apps as floating windows, which I found easy enough to handle productivity tasks that I struggle with on my phone's smaller screen. It's not really a replacement for a laptop, but at least you can get some work done at a pinch.

Lenovo included (almost) everything you could need

In a time when companies are trying to remove things from the included accessories, Lenovo stuffs the Legion Tab Gen 3 box with extras. You get a 68W fast charger, a USB-C to USB-C cable, a glass screen protector with tools for installation, and a folio case with a removable magnetic cover that helps vent extra heat from the tablet when used. I love seeing this, especially when cases or screen protectors might not always come from third-party vendors.

 Related
The Boox Note Air4 C is like an Android tablet combined with a Kindle, and it's everything I ever wanted

The idea of an Android-powered E Ink tablet changes everything

5

What I didn't like about the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3

It's got some odd decisions and it gets quite hot during long gaming sessions

Lenovo gets quite a lot right with this tablet, but some odd choices are at play here. I'm entirely convinced that nobody needs a dedicated macro lens on a tablet, let alone on a smartphone. The included folio is a nice touch, but it doesn't wrap around the screen as nicely as it could, or fold as nicely into a kickstand. Admittedly, these are minor quirks, especially when the rest of the tablet is pretty awesome. Oh, and there's no fingerprint scanner or secure face unlock, which is disappointing as those are fantastic features for easy access when you want to game.

It's got enough screen space for gaming or productivity without becoming a burden to carry around, and it feels premium in the hand

There is one other concern, and it's one that's mitigated somewhat by the metal chassis and the vented folio case. This tablet gets hot to the touch after a short while when gaming, often over 45 °C in my testing. That's not nice to hold with your fingertips, again making the case for using this tablet with a controller.

I'm not convinced this display needed to be 165Hz

The 8.8-inch display is smooth, bright, and gorgeous, and suspiciously similar to the specs of the Legion Go gaming handheld. So similar that I wouldn't be surprised to find it's the same panel, except the touchscreen works much better than it did on the Windows-powered handheld so it might be slightly different.

Either way, that's the only reason I can think that a 165Hz refresh rate was used for an Android tablet, because the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 isn't powerful enough for higher FPS, and most Android games are hard-capped at 60Hz or 120Hz. The list of high refresh capable games is still relatively short, so Lenovo could have cut costs somewhat by using a lesser specification. Unless, of course, it was using up old stock from the Legion Go.

 Related
Lenovo Tab Plus review: A surprisingly great budget Android tablet

This Android tablet is much better than its price suggests

3

Should you buy the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3?

You should buy the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 if:

  • You loved the Nexus 7 and wanted its successor
  • You use your tablet to play games
  • You want the option for productivity tasks in desktop mode

You should NOT buy the Lenovo Legion Tab 3 if:

  • You need a long battery life
  • You want a larger screen for media or getting work done
  • You want a fingerprint sensor for security

I believe Android tablets are better when they're smaller, and the Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 absolutely fits that bill. It's got plenty of power, a beautiful 8.8-inch screen with a higher refresh rate than you'll ever need, and can use a stylus for sketching and notetaking. It's even more usable now that the upgrade to Android 15 is here, and I feel this will be a firm favorite of Android fans.

It's even more usable now that the upgrade to Android 15 is here, and I feel this will be a firm favorite of Android fans.

It's got enough screen space for gaming or productivity without becoming a burden to carry around, and it feels premium in the hand. I'm not sure anyone buys tablets to use the cameras, but they're decent enough if you don't mind the odd color shift, and the audio system is loud and punchy. There is a successor on the horizon powered by the Snapdragon 8 Elite chip, so you might want to wait to see how pricing shakes out, but for now, this is the best small-screen Android tablet you can get.

Small, speedy, Android slate
Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3
8 / 10
$490 $550 Save $60

The Lenovo Legion Tab Gen 3 is a 8.8-inch Android tablet with a fast 165Hz refresh rate IPS screen, and plenty of grunt from the Snapdragon 8 Gen 3 SoC. The smaller screen makes it more usable for daily use, and it's gorgeous when playing games. Oh, and Lenovo includes a heat-dispersing folio case, glass screen protectors, and a 68W fast charger.

$490 at Lenovo

item.title: Microsoft will only get more persistant now that Copilot has plateaued
item.itunes:title: Microsoft will only get more persistant now that Copilot has plateaued
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you've been up to date with your Microsoft news, you'll know that the company recently reported that its Copilot user count has plateaued. The service attracts 20 million users a week, which sounds impressive on paper; however, given how ChatGPT garners 400 million users a week, you can see how things pale in comparison. Not only that, but given how roughly 1.5 billion users use Windows, and Copilot now comes as default with Windows 10 and 11, you'd think that number would be a tad higher. But it's not going up.

While that's good news for AI critics, this failure is likely to make Microsoft even more annoying. So, here's why Windows users should prep themselves for more Microsoft-based shenanigans in the future.

The advertisements for Copilot will ramp up

And will continue to do so until you use it

So, Copilot has stalled on user numbers. Guess it's time for Microsoft to pack it up, right? Admit that maybe they got off on the wrong foot and try another venture. Maybe use their partnership with OpenAI to allow ChatGPT to handle all the AI stuff while Microsoft does its own thing.

But we both know Microsoft is not going to do that. Like, really? This is the company that often doubles down on its choices, regardless of how people respond to them. That means that Copilot, Copilot Pro, and Copilot+ devices aren't going anywhere. And that means that Microsoft is going to advertise them even harder to get people to adopt their new AI overlords.

We're already kind of seeing it with Microsoft's monthly Windows 10 pester quest. Before the worrying Copilot statistic came out, Microsoft was content with just asking people on Windows 10 to purchase a Windows 11 machine to get them up to speed. However, the latest campaign specifically points out the benefits of a Copilot+ machine instead of a Windows 11 one. I think this is going to be Microsoft's main tactic as we head toward Windows 10's end-of-support date in October: advertising not just Windows 11, but the benefits of a Copilot+ device specifically.

 Related
5 reasons I completely remove Copilot on Windows 11 from all apps it’s crept into

Microsoft want to force Copilot AI into your apps on Windows, but I remove it for a better experience.l

6

The advertisements for other services are also going to ramp up

And will continue to do so even if you do use them

Alright, for this part, I'm going to need you to join me in the Speculation Zone. See, I believe Microsoft is currently looking for its big golden goose. Obviously, Windows does numbers for them; it's one of the most used operating systems in the world, after all. The problem is, after people buy a new computer with Windows or grab a Windows license, they can go for years without buying another one. Sometimes they won't purchase a new license until the newest version of Windows comes out. That's a lot of time waiting for money to come in.

So, the company has been on the hunt for a monthly money-maker. They have Microsoft 365 subscriptions, which are pretty good, but they need something else. When ChatGPT began to take off, I can imagine Microsoft seeing dollar signs when looking at its own assistant, Copilot. They could make special Copilot-enhanced devices (which make them money) that run Copilot locally and encourage people to upgrade to Copilot Pro (which makes them money) and perhaps then pay extra for the Office integration (which makes them money).

But now that golden egg hasn't panned out like Microsoft wants, and now it needs to find a way to help prop up the income while it tries to get Copilot off the ground. This means more ads for the Microsoft Store, more ads for its game store, and more ads for Microsoft 365. Oh, and let's not forget the ads within Copilot itself. If you thought things were bad now, I have a nasty feeling we're only just getting started with the ads.

 Related
5 features I wish were enabled by default on Windows 11

Windows 11 is packed with features but some of them should be enabled by default.

8

Using Windows is going to be annoying for the next few years

Regardless of how you feel about Copilot, one thing's for sure: Microsoft isn't going to take its lack of popularity lying down. Unfortunately for us, that means more pestering, both to use Copilot and to invest in its other products. And until the people finally accept the AI assistant into their lives like Microsoft wants, the advertisements will continue.

item.title: I use these 5 nifty tools to manage my self-hosted services, and I don't know how I'd live without them
item.itunes:title: I use these 5 nifty tools to manage my self-hosted services, and I don't know how I'd live without them
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Over the course of my self-hosting journey, I’ve encountered a diverse set of applications, each fulfilling its unique role in my rat’s nest of a home lab. There’s my gaming suite, which encompasses everything from Minecraft servers running on my NAS to the Apollo+Artemis combo on my gaming rig. I’ve also got my Raspberry Pi-powered productivity server, which rivals Office 365’s offerings without forcing me to pay monthly subscription fees. Likewise, I’ve configured a handful of virtual machines and containers for my coding projects and media consumption needs.

Then there’s the software stack that, despite not improving my productivity or coding skills, remains an essential part of my home lab by letting me manage, monitor, and organize my self-hosted services.

 Related
I built an always‑on VPN gateway with a Raspberry Pi Zero to access my home lab from anywhere

It's perfect when I want to work on my home lab projects from external networks

11

5 Watchtower

Automatic updates for your containerized services

Updating containers is quite different from installing newer versions of your typical PC app. Assuming you’ve mounted a storage volume to house the essential container files, you’ll need to stop the service and remove its container. After that, you’ll have to pull the updated image and use it to redeploy your container. While it may not sound like a big deal when you’ve only got a couple of containerized applications running on your system, updating a container stack filled with dozens of useful services can become a cumbersome process.

Watchtower can automate the entire update process, so you don’t have to waste hours manually arming your self-hosting workstation with new container images. Of course, there’s always the risk of Watchtower pulling a broken image and using it to deploy a malfunctioning container. Me? I use automatic updates for my low-priority containers and have configured Watchtower to notify me about newly-released images before using them to update my essential services (including the ones on this list).

4 Authentik

Make credential management a lot easier

Although my home server is a lot safer than the privacy nightmare that is my Windows 11 PC, I like to take several precautions to prevent unauthorized access to my self-hosted suite. Creating long passwords filled with meaningless strings and weird symbols is one such method, but there are times when even my reptile brain gets overloaded by Lovecraftian credentials.

A Single Sign-On authentication server like Authentik serves as a reliable cure for my security woes. Instead of managing dozens of usernames and passwords for my private app suite, I can get by with remembering just the credentials for my Authentik container. While it consumes more system resources than its rivals, Authentik supports multiple authorization protocols and includes a simple web UI that’s laden with several quality-of-life features.

3 Uptime Kuma

Perfect for monitoring your self-hosted stack

Despite doubling as amazing self-hosted workstations, home servers are experimental by their very nature. Whether it’s due to broken images, deleted config files, or botched experiments, it’s easy to bring down your home lab – and I speak from experience. Monitoring services are a must-have for every self-hosting enthusiast, as they can help you track the uptime and other statistics of your services.

While I often tinker with different monitoring solutions, Uptime Kuma remains my favorite application for the job. Since it’s lightweight and easy to deploy, I can spin up an Uptime Kuma container on a mere Raspberry Pi and connect my favorite services without dealing with complex YAML files and environment variables. Better yet, Uptime Kuma can send alerts via Discord, Telegram, and other apps the moment it detects an offline service in your self-hosting catalog.

2 Homepage

The most feature-rich dashboard out there

From Heimdall and Homarr to Dashy and Fenrus, I’ve tinkered with my fair share of dashboard applications ever since I began running containers and virtual machines, but most services lacked the necessary je ne sais quoi to remain as my permanent dashboard solution. For a home lab lover like myself, a dashboard isn’t just a way to easily access my software toolkit. It’s more of a means to showcase the full might of my imposing self-hosted stack to non-technical folks who wander into my computer-infested lair.

But after trying out Homepage last year, I finally switched from Heimdall and haven’t looked back even once. Difficulty-wise, Homepage has quite a learning curve, especially if you’re not accustomed to editing YAML files. However, it’s the most customizable dashboard utility I’ve ever used, and it can add an extra swagger to any home labber's setup once you outfit its config files with the right layouts, groups, backgrounds, bookmarks, and widgets.

1 Portainer

A web UI for your container fleet

The command line interfaces of Docker, Podman, and other container runtimes are undoubtedly the best way to manage your service stack if you’re a power user or a DevOps/sysadmin enthusiast. However, running long commands just to modify a single argument in your config files can get rather gruelling. That’s where container management platforms come in handy, as you don’t have to memorize multiple keywords to build your container lab.

Portainer is my favorite platform for controlling my containers. Despite featuring a simple menu-driven UI, Portainer lets you tinker with every aspect of your container suite, including storage mounts, app templates, and network configurations. Portainer also has dedicated tabs for the event logs, registries, and image repositories, and you can even use this nifty platform to manage your Docker Swarm and Kubernetes clusters.

Manage your self-hosted services like a pro by running these containers

Given the never-ending expanse of the home server ecosystem, there are multiple services that deserve a mention. A self-hosted WireGuard server can bolster the security of your connection when you need to access your home lab from an external network, while Tailscale is the better option for folks who suffer from CGNAT restrictions. If you’re as much of a Proxmox lover as I am, Proxmox Backup Server is worth checking out, as it can easily restore your PVE workstation should your experiments render it inoperable.

 Related
4 reasons why I'll stick with Proxmox even though the free version of ESXi is back

Why switch to ESXi again when Proxmox is just that good?

6

Monitoring services are a must-have for every self-hosting enthusiast, as they can help you track the uptime and other statistics of your services.

While I often tinker with different monitoring solutions, Uptime Kuma remains my favorite application for the job. Since it’s lightweight and easy to deploy, I can spin up an Uptime Kuma container on a mere Raspberry Pi and connect my favorite services without dealing with complex YAML files and environment variables. Better yet, Uptime Kuma can send alerts via Discord, Telegram, and other apps the moment it detects an offline service in your self-hosting catalog.

2 Homepage

The most feature-rich dashboard out there

From Heimdall and Homarr to Dashy and Fenrus, I’ve tinkered with my fair share of dashboard applications ever since I began running containers and virtual machines, but most services lacked the necessary je ne sais quoi to remain as my permanent dashboard solution. For a home lab lover like myself, a dashboard isn’t just a way to easily access my software toolkit. It’s more of a means to showcase the full might of my imposing self-hosted stack to non-technical folks who wander into my computer-infested lair.

But after trying out Homepage last year, I finally switched from Heimdall and haven’t looked back even once. Difficulty-wise, Homepage has quite a learning curve, especially if you’re not accustomed to editing YAML files. However, it’s the most customizable dashboard utility I’ve ever used, and it can add an extra swagger to any home labber's setup once you outfit its config files with the right layouts, groups, backgrounds, bookmarks, and widgets.

1 Portainer

A web UI for your container fleet

The command line interfaces of Docker, Podman, and other container runtimes are undoubtedly the best way to manage your service stack if you’re a power user or a DevOps/sysadmin enthusiast. However, running long commands just to modify a single argument in your config files can get rather gruelling. That’s where container management platforms come in handy, as you don’t have to memorize multiple keywords to build your container lab.

Portainer is my favorite platform for controlling my containers. Despite featuring a simple menu-driven UI, Portainer lets you tinker with every aspect of your container suite, including storage mounts, app templates, and network configurations. Portainer also has dedicated tabs for the event logs, registries, and image repositories, and you can even use this nifty platform to manage your Docker Swarm and Kubernetes clusters.

Manage your self-hosted services like a pro by running these containers

Given the never-ending expanse of the home server ecosystem, there are multiple services that deserve a mention. A self-hosted WireGuard server can bolster the security of your connection when you need to access your home lab from an external network, while Tailscale is the better option for folks who suffer from CGNAT restrictions. If you’re as much of a Proxmox lover as I am, Proxmox Backup Server is worth checking out, as it can easily restore your PVE workstation should your experiments render it inoperable.

 Related
4 reasons why I'll stick with Proxmox even though the free version of ESXi is back

Why switch to ESXi again when Proxmox is just that good?

6
item.content:encoded:
Over the course of my self-hosting journey, I’ve encountered a diverse set of applications, each fulfilling its unique role in my rat’s nest of a home lab. There’s my gaming suite, which encompasses everything from Minecraft servers running on my NAS to the Apollo+Artemis combo on my gaming rig. I’ve also got my Raspberry Pi-powered productivity server, which rivals Office 365’s offerings without forcing me to pay monthly subscription fees. Likewise, I’ve configured a handful of virtual machines and containers for my coding projects and media consumption needs.

Then there’s the software stack that, despite not improving my productivity or coding skills, remains an essential part of my home lab by letting me manage, monitor, and organize my self-hosted services.

 Related
I built an always‑on VPN gateway with a Raspberry Pi Zero to access my home lab from anywhere

It's perfect when I want to work on my home lab projects from external networks

11

5 Watchtower

Automatic updates for your containerized services

Updating containers is quite different from installing newer versions of your typical PC app. Assuming you’ve mounted a storage volume to house the essential container files, you’ll need to stop the service and remove its container. After that, you’ll have to pull the updated image and use it to redeploy your container. While it may not sound like a big deal when you’ve only got a couple of containerized applications running on your system, updating a container stack filled with dozens of useful services can become a cumbersome process.

Watchtower can automate the entire update process, so you don’t have to waste hours manually arming your self-hosting workstation with new container images. Of course, there’s always the risk of Watchtower pulling a broken image and using it to deploy a malfunctioning container. Me? I use automatic updates for my low-priority containers and have configured Watchtower to notify me about newly-released images before using them to update my essential services (including the ones on this list).

4 Authentik

Make credential management a lot easier

Although my home server is a lot safer than the privacy nightmare that is my Windows 11 PC, I like to take several precautions to prevent unauthorized access to my self-hosted suite. Creating long passwords filled with meaningless strings and weird symbols is one such method, but there are times when even my reptile brain gets overloaded by Lovecraftian credentials.

A Single Sign-On authentication server like Authentik serves as a reliable cure for my security woes. Instead of managing dozens of usernames and passwords for my private app suite, I can get by with remembering just the credentials for my Authentik container. While it consumes more system resources than its rivals, Authentik supports multiple authorization protocols and includes a simple web UI that’s laden with several quality-of-life features.

3 Uptime Kuma

Perfect for monitoring your self-hosted stack

Despite doubling as amazing self-hosted workstations, home servers are experimental by their very nature. Whether it’s due to broken images, deleted config files, or botched experiments, it’s easy to bring down your home lab – and I speak from experience. Monitoring services are a must-have for every self-hosting enthusiast, as they can help you track the uptime and other statistics of your services.

While I often tinker with different monitoring solutions, Uptime Kuma remains my favorite application for the job. Since it’s lightweight and easy to deploy, I can spin up an Uptime Kuma container on a mere Raspberry Pi and connect my favorite services without dealing with complex YAML files and environment variables. Better yet, Uptime Kuma can send alerts via Discord, Telegram, and other apps the moment it detects an offline service in your self-hosting catalog.

For a home lab lover like myself, a dashboard isn’t just a way to easily access my software toolkit. It’s more of a means to showcase the full might of my imposing self-hosted stack to non-technical folks who wander into my computer-infested lair.

But after trying out Homepage last year, I finally switched from Heimdall and haven’t looked back even once. Difficulty-wise, Homepage has quite a learning curve, especially if you’re not accustomed to editing YAML files. However, it’s the most customizable dashboard utility I’ve ever used, and it can add an extra swagger to any home labber's setup once you outfit its config files with the right layouts, groups, backgrounds, bookmarks, and widgets.

1 Portainer

A web UI for your container fleet

The command line interfaces of Docker, Podman, and other container runtimes are undoubtedly the best way to manage your service stack if you’re a power user or a DevOps/sysadmin enthusiast. However, running long commands just to modify a single argument in your config files can get rather gruelling. That’s where container management platforms come in handy, as you don’t have to memorize multiple keywords to build your container lab.

Portainer is my favorite platform for controlling my containers. Despite featuring a simple menu-driven UI, Portainer lets you tinker with every aspect of your container suite, including storage mounts, app templates, and network configurations. Portainer also has dedicated tabs for the event logs, registries, and image repositories, and you can even use this nifty platform to manage your Docker Swarm and Kubernetes clusters.

Manage your self-hosted services like a pro by running these containers

Given the never-ending expanse of the home server ecosystem, there are multiple services that deserve a mention. A self-hosted WireGuard server can bolster the security of your connection when you need to access your home lab from an external network, while Tailscale is the better option for folks who suffer from CGNAT restrictions. If you’re as much of a Proxmox lover as I am, Proxmox Backup Server is worth checking out, as it can easily restore your PVE workstation should your experiments render it inoperable.

 Related
4 reasons why I'll stick with Proxmox even though the free version of ESXi is back

Why switch to ESXi again when Proxmox is just that good?

6

item.title: Lenovo's new Legion 9i is bringing 3D gaming back to life
item.itunes:title: Lenovo's new Legion 9i is bringing 3D gaming back to life
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Lenovo Legion 9i Gen 10 brims with powerful hardware, including an RTX 5090.
  • For the first time, Lenovo is offering an optional glasses-free 3D display.
  • The new Legion 9i launches in China first, but it'll arrive in the US this fall.

Lenovo's Legion 9i was already the cream of the crop when it comes to high-end gaming laptops, and the next generation is pushing the envelope even further. Announced at Lenovo's Tech World event in Shanghai, the 10th-generation Legion 9i comes packed with the most powerful laptop hardware money can buy, including a mobile RTX 5090, Core Ultra 9 275HX, and up to 192GB of DDR5 memory. This generation, however, Lenovo also decided to pack in an optional 3D display with built-in eye tracking and a lenticular lens array.

Related
Acer SpatialLabs View Pro 27 review: An excellent and expensive glasses-free 3D solution for pros

It can give 2D photos and video a 3D effect, but this monitor works best with native 3D models and content.

The new Legion 9i looks insanely powerful

It's probably going to be insanely expensive, too

Glasses-free 3D is starting to gain some traction among gamers, and you don't need to look further than Samsung's recent Odyssey 3D to see that. Lenovo isn't borrowing the tech from a brand like Samsung or Acer, the latter of which has built up quite the impressive suite of 3D-enabled games through SpatialLabs. Lenovo is tackling the problem on its own. Through the Lenovo 3D Studio software, the company says you'll able to watch 3D videos in side-by-side or top-bottom formats, as well as play 30 supported games. Unfortunately, Lenovo hasn't revealed what those games are yet.

The display is interesting. Regardless of if you go with the 3D option or not, you're getting a 4K display with a 16:10 aspect ratio and a 240Hz refresh rate. Lenovo says this is a dual mode display, similar to the LG UltraGear Dual Mode OLED, and you can go between 4K at 240Hz and 1080p at 440Hz. If you choose the 3D display, you get the same specs. However, you'll also be able to swap over to a glasses-free 3D model that runs at half resolution. This is the same trick we've seen with other glasses-free 3D displays.

Outside the display, you get the fastest mobile hardware available right now, including the aforementioned RTX 5090 and space for up to four M.2 SSDs. This is an 18-inch laptop that basically defines the desktop replacement category, so it's not surprising to see so much horsepower on offer. Lenovo says the Legion 9i is able to supply a total of 280 watts to the CPU and GPU when they're under a full load, and it includes a massive 400W power adapter in the box to keep everything running smoothly.

Although the new Legion 9i sounds impressive, it's going to be expensive. The Gen 9 model will easily run you over $4,500, and that's not even with a top-end configuration. I wouldn't be surprised to see Lenovo ask for $6,000 (or more) for a tricked-out Gen 10 model with a 3D screen. Unfortunately, we'll have to wait to learn more on the pricing front. The new Legion 9i will launch first in China before coming to the US later this fall. Lenovo says it'll detail pricing and configuration options closer to release.

item.title: I turned a Raspberry Pi into a DNS server for my home network, and it's so useful
item.itunes:title: I turned a Raspberry Pi into a DNS server for my home network, and it's so useful
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
DNS is something you rarely think about or change in your internet connection. I was naive, too, and thought that merely changing it on my Windows PC would boost my connection. However, I didn't think deeply about the repercussions of not using a central DNS server with HTTPS until a few months back. When I learnt about the usefulness of creating a home DNS server with something as inexpensive as a Raspberry Pi, I pondered the time I would have saved in downloading ad blockers or hiding my web activities from the snoopy Internet Service Providers (ISPs).

Creating a Raspberry Pi-based DNS server requires little effort or terminal wizardry. You can use something like Dnsmasq for a simple DNS server and caching, or pick an advanced tool like Pi-Hole or AdGuard Home to store records and set up parental controls effectively. I'll discuss several benefits of converting one of my Raspberry Pi into a DNS server.

 Related
How I filter out all ads on all my devices with my NAS

Enjoy an ad-free online experience.

Shroud yourself from ISP tracking

Why allow blatant access to your web activity?

Whenever you get a new internet connection from a local service provider, they assign a custom DNS server to your router. I don't doubt their intent, but using their DNS server exposes all your web activity to them. But how? When you type those magical favorite URLs into your social media accounts, public forums, and websites, your computer doesn't automatically know where those URLs live. Each URL has a corresponding IP address that the DNS server tells your PC, so it can communicate with it and load the content.

So, the DNS server knows all the websites that you request and can use them for personal gain. It's like exposing your purchase history to all your neighbors, even when you don't want to. Data is practically a digital currency; you don't want to share it without consent. DNS servers are the easiest way to understand your usage patterns, and ISPs have a gala while exploiting them.

 Related
5 reasons I'm self-hosting a DNS server

Self-hosting DNS gave me local control, custom domains, and ad-blocking across my whole network.

11

Faster web browsing experience

No more slow load times and errors

Well, this experience can vary for many. I have heard from some of my friends that their ISPs' DNS servers resolve domain names faster than setting up a custom DNS server. Simply put, your browser gets the corresponding IP address from the DNS server and proceeds to the correct website. My experience hasn't been very good, and I have constantly faced slowdowns and errors while requesting some websites.

Changing the DNS server on my PC will only help it load the results faster. But using a centralized DNS server on a Pi gives all my devices (Phones, tablets, mini PCs, etc.) the same experience. I don't have to download the Cloudflare app on each one of them and set it up. Instead, I connect all of them to my Raspberry Pi's DNS server and get rid of potential slowdowns for good.

Personal Ad blocker at your service

Your home-wide ad blocker

Networkchuk once demonstrated a cool technique of typing a simple URL that loads the same, along with all the associated trackers. It's true for any popular website that you use. They all send a lot of trash your way.

I don't hate the ad idea in general, because websites need it to survive. But bundling a ton of useless ads in every nook and cranny of the page just when I asked for the website is also not okay.

A custom DNS server setup like Pi-Hole can weed out these trackers associated with a popular website and only send you the useful stuff. It's not a foolproof technique because these pesky people keep developing new tactics. But enthusiasts keep updating their blocklists, so it's not a rocky ride. Think of it like a mosquito net that blocks 90-95 percent of biting ninjas, but some still creep in, though not hurting you much.

Monitoring and controlling web activity wasn't this easy before

Enforce granular control on each device

You can maintain web activity records now that you have your own DNS server. It's a transparent way of checking what each device requests throughout the day and blocking any questionable stuff. Even if your kids/young siblings use a browser, most Google results often point to social media handles that open a treasure trove of content unsuitable for them.

I can add any of these popular websites to the blocklists, effectively protecting them on the web. The same is applicable for any sketchy site that lures them to play a game/watch something for free, force ads, and even try to squeeze personal information. Once it's on the blocklist, it's out of access for all the devices on my home network. I don't need to pick a device, go through web browser blocklists, or find an app or service for each one.

 Related
4 reasons you should use a custom DNS on your PC or router

If you're looking to optimize your internet experience, using a custom DNS can speed up your connection or block unwanted content

Custom DNS doesn't need to be expensive

Doesn't cost a dime after initial setup

Why pay for parental control software with recurring subscriptions when your custom DNS server can handle it for a small, one-time cost? Your tiny SBC, in this case an old model of Raspberry Pi, can sit beside your router—or even on the backside, if you want to keep it hidden. No one will know how the block is in place, and it barely sips any power.

Parental control apps are mostly sketchy and twist your arm to lure you into a high-tier subscription. Here, you get most of the control with a minimum investment at no additional cost. Your home devices will come inside a small protective net that becomes difficult to track, if not impossible.

Is a custom DNS server a necessity?

I don't have to stress more about why a custom DNS server is necessary. You get effective ad blocking, stay protected from snooping eyes, and save a lot while maintaining complete control over web activity. Moreover, multiple options exist, so you can pick, test, and then decide on one tool to configure and maintain your DNS server. Even if it's a little boost in load speed, the additional features are enough to convince you to create one now.
